name: Friendship
description: On loyalty, kindred spirits, and the friends who shape a life.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: For without friends no one would choose to live, though he had all other goods.
quotes.source: Nicomachean Ethics, Book VIII, Ch. 1 (c. 340 BC)
quotes.notes: W. D. Ross translation; the opening claim of Aristotle's account of philia.

quotes.content: Perfect friendship is the friendship of men who are good, and alike in virtue; for these wish well alike to each other qua good, and they are good themselves.
quotes.source: Nicomachean Ethics, Book VIII, Ch. 3 (c. 340 BC)
quotes.notes: W. D. Ross translation; Aristotle's highest of the three kinds of friendship.

quotes.content: For though the wish for friendship comes quickly, friendship does not.
quotes.source: Nicomachean Ethics, Book VIII, Ch. 3 (c. 340 BC)
quotes.notes: W. D. Ross translation; true friendship requires time and shared salt.

quotes.content: For his friend is another self.
quotes.source: Nicomachean Ethics, Book IX, Ch. 4 (c. 340 BC)
quotes.notes: W. D. Ross translation; the good man relates to his friend as to himself.

quotes.content: Friendship is nothing else than an accord in all things, human and divine, conjoined with mutual goodwill and affection.
quotes.notes: Falconer translation, Loeb Classical Library 1923; Cicero's formal definition of friendship.

quotes.content: For friendship adds a brighter radiance to prosperity and lessens the burden of adversity by dividing and sharing it.
quotes.notes: Falconer translation, Loeb Classical Library 1923; friendship doubles joy and halves grief.

quotes.content: Wherefore friends, though absent, are at hand; though in need, yet abound; though weak, are strong; and—harder saying still—though dead, are yet alive.
quotes.notes: Falconer translation, Loeb Classical Library 1923; friendship's power to hold the absent present.

quotes.content: If you press me to tell why I loved him, I feel that this cannot be expressed, except by answering: Because it was he, because it was I.
quotes.notes: Donald Frame translation; Montaigne on his friendship with Étienne de La Boétie.

quotes.content: Our souls mingle and blend with each other so completely that they efface the seam that joined them, and cannot find it again.
quotes.notes: Donald Frame translation; Montaigne's image of the perfect friendship as one soul.

quotes.content: A friend is a person with whom I may be sincere. Before him I may think aloud.
quotes.notes: One of the two 'elements' (Truth) Emerson names as the composition of friendship.

quotes.content: A friend may well be reckoned the masterpiece of nature.
quotes.notes: Emerson exalting friendship among nature's works.

quotes.content: The essence of friendship is entireness, a total magnanimity and trust.
quotes.notes: Emerson on the wholeness demanded by true friendship.

quotes.content: Friendship requires that rare mean betwixt likeness and unlikeness, that piques each with the presence of power and of consent in the other party.
quotes.notes: Emerson on friendship as a balance of sameness and difference.

quotes.content: The only reward of virtue is virtue; the only way to have a friend is to be one.
quotes.notes: The full sentence behind the famous 'to have a friend, be one'; from the essay's close.

quotes.content: Your friend is your needs answered. He is your field which you sow with love and reap with thanksgiving.
quotes.notes: Opening of the Prophet's answer when a youth asks him to speak of friendship.

quotes.content: When you part from your friend, you grieve not; For that which you love most in him may be clearer in his absence, as the mountain to the climber is clearer from the plain.
quotes.notes: Gibran on distance clarifying what we love in a friend.

quotes.content: Seek him always with hours to live. For it is his to fill your need, but not your emptiness.
quotes.notes: Gibran warning against treating a friend as a mere killer of idle time.

quotes.content: And in the sweetness of friendship let there be laughter, and sharing of pleasures. For in the dew of little things the heart finds its morning and is refreshed.
quotes.notes: The closing lines of Gibran's meditation on friendship.

quotes.content: Friendship is born at the moment when one man says to another 'What! You too? I thought that no one but myself . . .'
quotes.notes: Lewis on the flash of recognition that turns companionship into friendship.

quotes.content: Friendship is unnecessary, like philosophy, like art, like the universe itself (for God did not need to create). It has no survival value; rather it is one of those things which give value to survival.
quotes.notes: Lewis defending friendship as gloriously superfluous to mere survival.

quotes.content: Each friend represents a world in us, a world possibly not born until they arrive, and it is only by this meeting that a new world is born.
quotes.source: The Diary of Anaïs Nin, Vol. 1 (1966)
quotes.notes: From the first published volume of Nin's diaries (entry period 1931–1934).

quotes.content: She is a friend of my mind. She gather me, man. The pieces I am, she gather them and give them back to me in all the right order.
quotes.notes: Sixo describing the Thirty-Mile Woman; one of the novel's images of restorative love.

quotes.content: My friends have made the story of my life. In a thousand ways they have turned my limitations into beautiful privileges.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the friends and teachers who shaped Keller's life.

quotes.content: I do then with my friends as I do with my books. I would have them where I can find them, but I seldom use them.
quotes.notes: Emerson on prizing friends while guarding his own solitude and independence.

quotes.content: Friendship is a single soul dwelling in two bodies.
quotes.source: Attributed via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Eminent Philosophers
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: not found in the Nicomachean Ethics; attributed to Aristotle only by Diogenes Laërtius (3rd c. AD), wording varies by translation.

quotes.content: Walking with a friend in the dark is better than walking alone in the light.
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: widely attributed to Keller across aggregators but no book, essay, or letter has been located.

quotes.content: Friendship is not something you learn in school. But if you haven't learned the meaning of friendship, you really haven't learned anything.
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: circulates only on quote aggregators with no cited speech, interview, or book.

quotes.content: A real friend is one who walks in when the rest of the world walks out.
quotes.source: Attributed; popularized in his newspaper column
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: per Quote Investigator, an evolving adage (earlier forms 1924/1933) that Winchell popularized but did not originate.
